attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking: I'm looking at Cal.com's structure to get a better sense of how it's organized.

content.content:
{
  "name": "calcom-monorepo",
  "version": "0.0.0",
  "private": true,
  "workspaces": [
    "apps/*",
    "apps/api/*",
    "packages/*",
    "packages/embeds/*",
    "packages/features/*",
    "packages/app-store",
    "packages/app-store/*",
    "packages/platform/*",
    "packages/platform/examples/base",
    "example-apps/*"
  ],
  "scripts": {
    "app-store-cli": "yarn workspace @calcom/app-store-cli",
    "app-store:build": "yarn turbo build --filter=@calcom/app-store-cli",
    "app-store:watch": "yarn app-store-cli watch",
    "app-store": "yarn app-store-cli cli",
    "create-app": "yarn app-store create",
    "edit-app": "yarn app-store edit",
    "withEmbedPublishEnv": "NEXT_PUBLIC_EMBED_LIB_URL='https://app.cal.com/embed/embed.js' NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL='https://app.cal.com' yarn",
    "publish-embed": "yarn withEmbedPublishEnv workspace @calcom/embed-core build && yarn withEmbedPublishEnv workspace @calcom/embed-snippet build && yarn workspaces foreach --from=\"@calcom/embed*\" npm publish --access=public",
    "delete-app": "yarn app-store delete",
    "create-app-template": "yarn app-store create-template",
    "edit-app-template": "yarn app-store edit-template",
    "delete-app-template": "yarn app-store delete-template",
    "build": "turbo run build --filter=@calcom/web...",
    "build:ai": "turbo run build --filter=\"@calcom/ai\"",
    "clean": "find . -name node_modules -o -name .next -o -name .turbo -o -name dist -type d -prune | xargs rm -rf",
    "db-deploy": "turbo run db-deploy",
    "db-seed": "turbo run db-seed",
    "db-studio": "yarn prisma studio",
    "deploy": "turbo run deploy",
    "dev:all": "turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/web\" --filter=\"@calcom/website\" --filter=\"@calcom/console\"",
    "dev:ai": "turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/web\" --filter=\"@calcom/api-proxy\" --filter=\"@calcom/ai\"",
    "dev:api": "turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/web\" --filter=\"@calcom/api-proxy\"",
    "dev:api:console": "turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/web\" --filter=\"@calcom/api-proxy\" --filter=\"@calcom/console\"",
    "dev:console": "turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/web\" --filter=\"@calcom/console\"",
    "dev:swagger": "turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/api-proxy\" --filter=\"@calcom/swagger\"",
    "dev:website": "turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/web\" --filter=\"@calcom/website\"",
    "dev:trigger": "turbo run dev:trigger --filter=\"@calcom/features\"",
    "deploy:trigger:prod": "turbo run deploy:trigger:prod --filter=\"@calcom/features\"",
    "deploy:trigger:staging": "turbo run deploy:trigger:staging --filter=\"@calcom/features\"",
    "dev": "turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/web\"",
    "dx": "turbo run dx",
    "i-dev": "infisical run -- turbo run dev --filter=\"@calcom/web\"",
    "i-dx": "infisical run -- turbo run dx",
    "i-gen-web-example-env": "infisical secrets generate-example-env --tags=web > .env.example",
    "i-gen-app-store-example-env": "infisical secrets generate-example-env --tags=appstore > .env.appStore.example",
    "embed-tests-quick": "turbo run embed-tests-quick",
    "embed-tests": "turbo run embed-tests",
    "env-check:app-store": "dotenv-checker --schema .env.appStore.example --env .env.appStore",
    "env-check:common": "dotenv-checker --schema .env.example --env .env",
    "format": "biome format --write .",
    "heroku-postbuild": "turbo run @calcom/web#build",
    "lint:fix": "turbo lint:fix",
    "lint:report": "turbo lint:report",
    "lint": "turbo lint",
    "postinstall": "husky install && turbo run post-install",
    "pre-commit": "lint-staged",
    "predev": "echo 'Checking env files'",
    "prisma": "yarn workspace @calcom/prisma prisma",
    "start": "turbo run start --filter=\"@calcom/web\"",
    "tdd": "vitest watch",
    "e2e": "NEXT_PUBLIC_IS_E2E=1 yarn playwright test --project=@calcom/web",
    "e2e:app-store": "NEXT_PUBLIC_IS_E2E=1 QUICK=true yarn playwright test --project=@calcom/app-store",
    "e2e:embed": "NEXT_PUBLIC_IS_E2E=1 yarn playwright test --project=@calcom/embed-core",
    "e2e:embed-react": "QUICK=true yarn playwright test --project=@calcom/embed-react",
    "test-e2e": "yarn db-seed && yarn e2e",
    "test-e2e:app-store": "yarn db-seed && yarn e2e:app-store",
    "test-e2e:embed": "yarn db-seed && yarn e2e:embed",
    "test-e2e:embed-react": "yarn db-seed && yarn e2e:embed-react",
    "test-playwright": "yarn playwright test --config=playwright.config.ts",
    "test": "TZ=UTC vitest run",
    "test:ui": "TZ=UTC vitest --ui",
    "type-check": "turbo run type-check",
    "type-check:ci": "turbo run type-check:ci --log-prefix=none",
    "web": "yarn workspace @calcom/web",
    "changesets-add": "yarn changeset add",
    "changesets-version": "yarn changeset version",
    "changesets-release": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max_old_space_size=12288' turbo run build-npm --filter=@calcom/atoms && yarn changeset publish",
    "lint-staged": "lint-staged"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@biomejs/biome": "2.3.10",
    "@changesets/changelog-github": "0.5.1",
    "@changesets/cli": "2.29.4",
    "@faker-js/faker": "9.2.0",
    "@jetstreamapp/soql-parser-js": "6.1.0",
    "@playwright/test": "1.57.0",
    "@prisma/internals": "6.16.2",
    "@snaplet/copycat": "4.1.0",
    "@testing-library/jest-dom": "5.17.0",
    "@testing-library/react": "16.0.1",
    "@vitest/coverage-v8": "4.0.16",
    "@vitest/ui": "4.0.16",
    "c8": "7.13.0",

  "name": "@calcom/web",
  "version": "6.2.0",
  "private": true,
  "scripts": {
    "analyze": "ANALYZE=true next build",
    "analyze:server": "BUNDLE_ANALYZE=server next build",
    "analyze:browser": "BUNDLE_ANALYZE=browser next build",
    "clean": "rm -rf .turbo && rm -rf node_modules && rm -rf .next",
    "dev": "turbo run copy-app-store-static && next dev --turbopack",
    "dev:scan": "yarn dev & npx --yes react-scan@latest localhost:3000",
    "dev:cron": "npx tsx cron-tester.ts",
    "dev-https": "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 next dev --experimental-https",
    "dx": "yarn dev",
    "test-codegen": "yarn playwright codegen http://localhost:3000",
    "type-check": "tsc --pretty --noEmit",
    "type-check:ci": "tsc-absolute --pretty --noEmit",
    "sentry:release": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max-old-space-size=6144' node scripts/create-sentry-release.js",
    "copy-static": "turbo run copy-app-store-static",
    "copy-app-store-static": "node scripts/copy-app-store-static.js",
    "build": "next build && yarn sentry:release",
    "start": "next start",
    "lint": "biome lint .",
    "lint:fix": "biome lint --write .",

    "translate-locales": "ts-node scripts/check-missing-translations.ts",
    "stripe:listen": "stripe listen --forward-to http://localhost:3000/api/stripe/webhook"

    "yarn": "3.4.1"

---
{
  "name": "@calcom/api-v2",
  "version": "0.0.1",
  "description": "Platform API for Cal.diy",
  "author": "Cal.com, Inc.",
  "license": "UNLICENSED",
  "private": true,
  "scripts": {
    "build": "nest build",
    "build:docker": "yarn workspace @calcom/platform-constants build && yarn workspace @calcom/platform-enums build && yarn workspace @calcom/platform-utils build && yarn workspace @calcom/platform-types build && yarn workspace @calcom/platform-libraries build && yarn workspace @calcom/trpc build:server",
    "format": "biome format --write src test",
    "start": "nest start",
    "dev:build:watch": "concurrently --names \"libraries,lru-fix,constants,enums,utils,types\" \"yarn _dev:build:watch:libraries\" \"yarn _dev:build:watch:libraries:lru-fix\" \"yarn _dev:build:watch:constants\" \"yarn _dev:build:watch:enums\" \"yarn _dev:build:watch:utils\" \"yarn _dev:build:watch:types\"",
    "_dev:build:watch:libraries": "yarn workspace @calcom/platform-libraries build:watch",
    "_dev:build:watch:libraries:lru-fix": "yarn workspace @calcom/platform-libraries watch-lru-fix",
    "_dev:build:watch:constants": "yarn workspace @calcom/platform-constants build:watch",
    "_dev:build:watch:enums": "yarn workspace @calcom/platform-enums build:watch",
    "_dev:build:watch:utils": "yarn workspace @calcom/platform-utils build:watch",
    "_dev:build:watch:types": "yarn workspace @calcom/platform-types build:watch",
    "dev:build": "yarn workspace @calcom/platform-constants build && yarn workspace @calcom/platform-enums build && yarn workspace @calcom/platform-utils build && yarn workspace @calcom/platform-types build && yarn workspace @calcom/platform-libraries build && yarn workspace @calcom/trpc build:server",
    "dev": "yarn dev:build && ts-node scripts/docker-start.ts && yarn copy-swagger-module && yarn start --watch",
    "dev:no-docker": "yarn dev:build && yarn copy-swagger-module && yarn start --watch",
    "start:debug": "nest start --debug --watch",
    "start:prod": "node ./dist/apps/api/v2/src/main.js",
    "test": "yarn dev:build && jest",
    "test:watch": "yarn dev:build && jest --watch",
    "test:cov": "yarn dev:build && jest --coverage",
    "test:debug": "node --inspect-brk -r tsconfig-paths/register -r ts-node/register node_modules/.bin/jest --runInBand",
    "test:e2e": "yarn dev:build && NODE_OPTIONS='--max_old_space_size=8192 --experimental-vm-modules' jest --ci --forceExit --config ./jest-e2e.ts",
    "test:e2e:ci": "NODE_OPTIONS='--max_old_space_size=8192 --experimental-vm-modules' jest --ci --forceExit --config ./jest-e2e.ts",
    "test:e2e:local": "yarn test:e2e --maxWorkers=4",
    "test:e2e:watch": "yarn dev:build && jest --runInBand --detectOpenHandles --forceExit --config ./jest-e2e.ts --watch",
    "prisma": "yarn workspace @calcom/prisma prisma",
    "generate-schemas": "yarn prisma generate && yarn prisma format",
    "copy-swagger-module": "ts-node -r tsconfig-paths/register src/swagger/copy-swagger-module.ts",
    "generate-swagger": "yarn copy-swagger-module && yarn build && node ./dist/apps/api/v2/src/swagger/generate-swagger-script.js",
    "generate-secrets": "ts-node scripts/generate-secrets.ts",
    "prepare": "yarn run snyk-protect",
    "snyk-protect": "snyk-protect"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@axiomhq/winston": "1.2.0",
    "@calcom/platform-constants": "workspace:*",
    "@calcom/platform-enums": "workspace:*",
    "@calcom/platform-libraries": "workspace:*",
    "@calcom/platform-types": "workspace:*",
    "@calcom/platform-utils": "workspace:*",
    "@calcom/prisma": "workspace:*",
    "@microsoft/microsoft-graph-types-beta": "0.42.0-preview",
    "@nest-lab/throttler-storage-redis": "1.0.0",

content.content:
# ********** INDEX **********
#
# - LICENSE
# - DATABASE
# - SHARED
#   - NEXTAUTH
# - E-MAIL SETTINGS
# - ORGANIZATIONS

# - LICENSE ************************************************************************************
# This project is licensed under the MIT License.
# @see https://github.com/calcom/cal.diy/blob/main/LICENSE
# Cal.diy is fully open source — no license key is required.
# ***********************************************************************************************************

# - DATABASE ************************************************************************************************
DATABASE_URL="postgresql://postgres:@localhost:5450/calendso"
# Needed to run migrations while using a connection pooler like PgBouncer
# Use the same one as DATABASE_URL if you're not using a connection pooler
DATABASE_DIRECT_URL="postgresql://postgres:@localhost:5450/calendso"
INSIGHTS_DATABASE_URL=

# ***********************************************************************************************************

# - SHARED **************************************************************************************************
# Set this to http://app.cal.local:3000 if you want to enable organizations, and
# check variable ORGANIZATIONS_ENABLED at the bottom of this file
NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL='http://localhost:3000'
# Change to 'http://localhost:3001' if running the website simultaneously
NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_URL='http://localhost:3000'
NEXT_PUBLIC_EMBED_LIB_URL='http://localhost:3000/embed/embed.js'

# To enable SAML login, set both these variables
# @see https://github.com/calcom/cal.diy/tree/main/packages/features/ee#setting-up-saml-login
# SAML_DATABASE_URL="postgresql://postgres:@localhost:5450/cal-saml"
SAML_DATABASE_URL=
# SAML_ADMINS='pro@example.com'
SAML_ADMINS=
# NEXT_PUBLIC_HOSTED_CAL_FEATURES=1
NEXT_PUBLIC_HOSTED_CAL_FEATURES=

# If you use Heroku to deploy Postgres (or use self-signed certs for Postgres) then uncomment the follow line.
# @see https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/connecting-heroku-postgres#connecting-in-node-js
# PGSSLMODE='no-verify'
PGSSLMODE=

# Define which hostnames are expected for the app to work on
ALLOWED_HOSTNAMES='"cal.local:3000","localhost:3000"'
#  Reserved orgs subdomains for our own usage
RESERVED_SUBDOMAINS='"app","auth","docs","design","console","go","status","api","saml","www","matrix","developer","cal","my","team","support","security","blog","learn","admin"'

#   - NEXTAUTH
# @see: https://github.com/calendso/calendso/issues/263
# @see: https://next-auth.js.org/configuration/options#nextauth_url
# Required for Vercel hosting - set NEXTAUTH_URL to equal your NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL
NEXTAUTH_URL='http://localhost:3000'
# @see: https://next-auth.js.org/configuration/options#nextauth_secret
# You can use: `openssl rand -base64 32` to generate one
NEXTAUTH_SECRET=
# Used for cross-domain cookie authentication
NEXTAUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN=

# Set this to '1' if you don't want Cal to collect anonymous usage
CALCOM_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=

# ApiKey for cronjobs
CRON_API_KEY='0cc0e6c35519bba620c9360cfe3e68d0'

# Whether to automatically keep app metadata in the database in sync with the metadata/config files. When disabled, the
# sync runs in a reporting-only dry-run mode.
CRON_ENABLE_APP_SYNC=false

# Application Key for symmetric encryption and decryption
# must be 32 bytes for AES256 encryption algorithm
# You can use: `openssl rand -base64 24` to generate a 32-character key
CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY=

# Intercom Config
NEXT_PUBLIC_INTERCOM_APP_ID=

# Secret to enable Intercom Identity Verification
INTERCOM_SECRET=

# Intercom api token for free users
INTERCOM_API_TOKEN=

# Posthog Config
NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_KEY=

NEXT_PUBLIC_POSTHOG_HOST=



# Dub Config
DUB_API_KEY=
NEXT_PUBLIC_DUB_PROGRAM_ID=
# Optional: Domain for SMS workflow shortened links
DUB_SMS_DOMAIN=
# Optional: Folder ID to organize SMS workflow shortened links in Dub
DUB_SMS_FOLDER_ID=

content.content:
> [!WARNING] > Use at your own risk. Cal.diy is the open source community edition of Cal.com and it is intended for users who want to self-host their own Cal.diy instance. It is strictly recommended for personal, non-production use. Please review all installation and configuration steps carefully. Self-hosting requires advanced knowledge of server administration, database management, and securing sensitive data. Proceed only if you are comfortable with these responsibilities. > [!TIP] > For any commercial and enterprise-ready scheduling infrastructure, use Cal.com, not Cal.diy; hosted by us or get invited to on-prem enterprise access here: https://cal.com/sales

Cal.diy

The community-driven, open-source scheduling platform.
GitHub

Discussions · Issues · Contributing

## About Cal.diy **Cal.diy** is the community-driven, fully open-source scheduling platform — a fork of [Cal.com](https://cal.com) with all enterprise/commercial code removed. Cal.diy is **100% MIT-licensed** with no proprietary "Enterprise Edition" features. It's designed for individuals and self-hosters who want full control over their scheduling infrastructure without any commercial dependencies. ### What's different from Cal.com? - **No enterprise features** — Teams, Organizations, Insights, Workflows, SSO/SAML, and other EE-only features have been removed - **No license key required** — Everything works out of the box, no Cal.com account or license needed - **100% open source** — The entire codebase is licensed under MIT, no "Open Core" split - **Community-maintained** — Contributions are welcome and go directly into this project (see [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md)) > **Note:** Cal.diy is a self-hosted project. There is no hosted/managed version. You run it on your own infrastructure. ### Built With - [Next.js](https://nextjs.org/) - [tRPC](https://trpc.io/) - [React.js](https://reactjs.org/) - [Tailwind CSS](https://tailwindcss.com/) - [Prisma.io](https://prisma.io/) - [Daily.co](https://daily.co/) ## Getting Started To get a local copy up and running, please follow these simple steps. ### Prerequisites Here’s what you need to run Cal.diy. - Node.js (Version: >=18.x) - PostgreSQL (Version: >=13.x) - Yarn _(recommended)_ > If you want to enable any of the available integrations, you may want to obtain additional credentials for each one. More details on this can be found below under the [integrations section](#integrations). ## Development ### Setup 1. Clone the repo (or fork https://github.com/calcom/cal.diy/fork) ```sh git clone https://github.com/calcom/cal.diy.git ``` > If you are on Windows, run the following command in Git Bash with admin privileges: > `git clone -c core.symlinks=true https://github.com/calcom/cal.diy.git` 2. Go to the project folder ```sh cd cal.diy ```

content.content:
3. Install packages with yarn

   ```sh
   yarn
   ```

4. Set up your `.env` file

   - Duplicate `.env.example` to `.env`
   - Use `openssl rand -base64 32` to generate a key and add it under `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` in the `.env` file.
   - Use `openssl rand -base64 24` to generate a key and add it under `CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY` in the `.env` file.

 > **Windows users:** Replace the `packages/prisma/.env` symlink with a real copy to avoid a Prisma error (`unexpected character / in variable name`):
 >
 > ```sh
 > # Git Bash / WSL
 > rm packages/prisma/.env && cp .env packages/prisma/.env
 > ```

5. Set up Node
   If your Node version does not meet the project's requirements as instructed by the docs, "nvm" (Node Version Manager) allows using Node at the version required by the project:

   ```sh
   nvm use
   ```

   You first might need to install the specific version and then use it:

   ```sh
   nvm install && nvm use
   ```

   You can install nvm from [here](https://github.com/nvm-sh/nvm).

#### Quick start with `yarn dx`

> - **Requires Docker and Docker Compose to be installed**
> - Will start a local Postgres instance with a few test users - the credentials will be logged in the console

```sh
yarn dx
```

**Default credentials created:**

| Email | Password | Role |
|-------|----------|------|
| `free@example.com` | `free` | Free user |
| `pro@example.com` | `pro` | Pro user |
| `trial@example.com` | `trial` | Trial user |
| `admin@example.com` | `ADMINadmin2022!` | Admin user |
| `onboarding@example.com` | `onboarding` | Onboarding incomplete |

You can use any of these credentials to sign in at [http://localhost:3000](http://localhost:3000)

> **Tip**: To view the full list of seeded users and their details, run `yarn db-studio` and visit [http://localhost:5555](http://localhost:5555)

#### Development tip

1. Add `export NODE_OPTIONS="--max-old-space-size=16384"` to your shell script to increase the memory limit for the node process. Alternatively, you can run this in your terminal before running the app. Replace 16384 with the amount of RAM you want to allocate to the node process.

2. Add `NEXT_PUBLIC_LOGGER_LEVEL={level}` to your .env file to control the logging verbosity for all tRPC queries and mutations.\
   Where {level} can be one of the following:

   `0` for silly \
   `1` for trace \
   `2` for debug \
   `3` for info \
   `4` for warn \
   `5` for error \
   `6` for fatal

   When you set `NEXT_PUBLIC_LOGGER_LEVEL={level}` in your .env file, it enables logging at that level and higher. Here's how it works:

   The logger will include all logs that are at the specified level or higher. For example: \

   - If you set `NEXT_PUBLIC_LOGGER_LEVEL=2`, it will log from level 2 (debug) upwards, meaning levels 2 (debug), 3 (info), 4 (warn), 5 (error), and 6 (fatal) will be logged. \
   - If you set `NEXT_PUBLIC_LOGGER_LEVEL=3`, it will log from level 3 (info) upwards, meaning levels 3 (info), 4 (warn), 5 (error), and 6 (fatal) will be logged, but level 2 (debug) and level 1 (trace) will be ignored. \

```sh
echo 'NEXT_PUBLIC_LOGGER_LEVEL=3' >> .env
```

for Logger level to be set at info, for example.

#### Gitpod Setup

1. Click the button below to open this project in Gitpod.

2. This will open a fully configured workspace in your browser with all the necessary dependencies already installed.

[![Open in Gitpod](https://gitpod.io/button/open-in-gitpod.svg)](https://gitpod.io/#https://github.com/calcom/cal.diy)

#### Manual setup

1. Configure environment variables in the `.env` file. Replace `<user>`, `<pass>`, `<db-host>`, and `<db-port>` with their applicable values

   ```
   DATABASE_URL='postgresql://<user>:<pass>@<db-host>:<db-port>'
   ```

   <details>
   <summary>If you don't know how to configure the DATABASE_URL, then follow the steps here to create a quick local DB</summary>

   1. [Download](https://www.postgresql.org/download/) and install PostgreSQL locally (if you don't have it already).

   2. Create your own local db by executing `createDB <DB name>`

   3. Now open your psql shell with the DB you created: `psql -h localhost -U postgres -d <DB name>`

   4. Inside the psql shell execute `\conninfo`. And you will get the following info.
      ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/39329182/236612291-51d87f69-6dc1-4a23-bf4d-1ca1754e0a35.png)

   5. Now extract all the info and add it to your DATABASE_URL. The url would look something like this
      `postgresql://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/Your-DB-Name`. The port is configurable and does not have to be 5432.

   </details>

   If you don't want to create a local DB. Then you can also consider using services like railway.app, Northflank or render.

   - [Setup postgres DB with railway.app](https://docs.railway.app/guides/postgresql)
   - [Setup postgres DB with Northflank](https://northflank.com/guides/deploy-postgres-database-on-northflank)
   - [Setup postgres DB with render](https://render.com/docs/databases)

2. Copy and paste your `DATABASE_URL` from `.env` to `.env.appStore`.

3. Set up the database using the Prisma schema (found in `packages/prisma/schema.prisma`)

   In a development environment, run:

   ```sh
   yarn workspace @calcom/prisma db-migrate
   ```

   In a production environment, run:

   ```sh
   yarn workspace @calcom/prisma db-deploy
   ```

4. Run [mailhog](https://github.com/mailhog/MailHog) to view emails sent during development

   > **_NOTE:_** Required when `E2E_TEST_MAILHOG_ENABLED` is "1"

   ```sh
   docker pull mailhog/mailhog
   docker run -d -p 8025:8025 -p 1025:1025 mailhog/mailhog
   ```

5. Run (in development mode)

   ```sh
   yarn dev
   ```

#### Setting up your first user

##### Approach 1

1. Open [Prisma Studio](https://prisma.io/studio) to look at or modify the database content:

content.content:
#!/bin/sh

content.content:
# this file is a helper to run Cal.diy locally
# starts a postgres 18 instance on port 5450 to use as a local db
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    ports:
      - "5450:5432" # expose pg on port 5450 to not collide with pg from elsewhere
    restart: always
    volumes:
      - db_data:/var/lib/postgresql
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: "cal-saml"
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ""
      POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD: trust
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
  postgres_is_ready:
    image: postgres
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
volumes:
  db_data:

content.content:
FROM --platform=$BUILDPLATFORM node:20 AS builder

WORKDIR /calcom

## If we want to read any ENV variable from .env file, we need to first accept and pass it as an argument to the Dockerfile
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_LICENSE_CONSENT
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_TERMS_URL
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_PRIVACY_POLICY_URL
ARG CALCOM_TELEMETRY_DISABLED
ARG DATABASE_URL
ARG NEXTAUTH_SECRET=secret
ARG CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY=secret
ARG MAX_OLD_SPACE_SIZE=6144
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_API_V2_URL
ARG CSP_POLICY

## We need these variables as required by Next.js build to create rewrites
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SINGLE_ORG_SLUG
ARG ORGANIZATIONS_ENABLED

ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL=http://NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL_PLACEHOLDER \
  NEXT_PUBLIC_API_V2_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_API_V2_URL \
  NEXT_PUBLIC_LICENSE_CONSENT=$NEXT_PUBLIC_LICENSE_CONSENT \
  NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_TERMS_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_TERMS_URL \
  NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_PRIVACY_POLICY_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_PRIVACY_POLICY_URL \
  CALCOM_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=$CALCOM_TELEMETRY_DISABLED \
  DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL \
  DATABASE_DIRECT_URL=$DATABASE_URL \
  NEXTAUTH_SECRET=${NEXTAUTH_SECRET} \
  CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY=${CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY} \
  NEXT_PUBLIC_SINGLE_ORG_SLUG=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SINGLE_ORG_SLUG \
  ORGANIZATIONS_ENABLED=$ORGANIZATIONS_ENABLED \
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=${MAX_OLD_SPACE_SIZE} \
  BUILD_STANDALONE=true \
  CSP_POLICY=$CSP_POLICY

COPY package.json yarn.lock .yarnrc.yml playwright.config.ts turbo.json i18n.json ./
COPY .yarn ./.yarn
COPY apps/web ./apps/web
COPY apps/api/v2 ./apps/api/v2
COPY packages ./packages

RUN yarn config set httpTimeout 1200000
RUN npx turbo prune --scope=@calcom/web --scope=@calcom/trpc --docker
RUN yarn install
# Build and make embed servable from web/public/embed folder
RUN yarn workspace @calcom/trpc run build
RUN yarn --cwd packages/embeds/embed-core workspace @calcom/embed-core run build
RUN yarn --cwd apps/web workspace @calcom/web run copy-app-store-static
RUN yarn --cwd apps/web workspace @calcom/web run build
RUN rm -rf node_modules/.cache .yarn/cache apps/web/.next/cache

FROM node:20 AS builder-two

WORKDIR /calcom
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL=http://localhost:3000

ENV NODE_ENV=production

COPY package.json .yarnrc.yml turbo.json i18n.json ./
COPY .yarn ./.yarn
COPY --from=builder /calcom/yarn.lock ./yarn.lock
COPY --from=builder /calcom/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY --from=builder /calcom/packages ./packages
COPY --from=builder /calcom/apps/web ./apps/web
COPY --from=builder /calcom/packages/prisma/schema.prisma ./prisma/schema.prisma
COPY scripts scripts
RUN chmod +x scripts/*

# Save value used during this build stage. If NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL and BUILT_NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL differ at
# run-time, then start.sh will find/replace static values again.
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL \
  BUILT_NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL

RUN scripts/replace-placeholder.sh http://NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL_PLACEHOLDER ${NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL}

FROM node:20 AS runner

WORKDIR /calcom

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends netcat-openbsd wget && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

COPY --from=builder-two /calcom ./
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL=http://localhost:3000
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL \
  BUILT_NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL

ENV NODE_ENV=production
EXPOSE 3000

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=30s --retries=5 \
  CMD wget --spider http://localhost:3000 || exit 1

CMD ["/calcom/scripts/start.sh"]

lastPrompt: Alloy, setup our workspace please! ✨🚀    <mode>ACT</mode>

content.thinking:
I'm looking at a massive Cal.com monorepo with NextJS frontend and NestJS backend that has a slow multi-stage Docker build, so I need to create a lightweight development setup that runs on host network mode with the frontend accessible at localhost:8080 without exposing individual ports.

The Next.js app defaults to port 3000, so I'll need to override it with `next dev --turbopack --port 8080`, but that means I also need to set the NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL environment variable to match. My pragmatic approach is to spin up the minimal services needed: the Next.js web app, Postgres for the database, and Redis, all on the host network. Given the massive dependency tree and compile time, this will be slow initially but should be workable for validation purposes.

content.thinking:
Setting up the Docker Compose configuration with a multi-service architecture, starting with Postgres on the host network with a trust authentication method and persistent data volume.

Configuring the Node.js web service to run on the host network as well, mounting the workspace and node_modules volumes, then setting up all the necessary environment variables for the Next.js application including database connections, authentication secrets, and telemetry settings...

Actually, I'm realizing there's a problem with how the port gets passed to the Next.js dev server. The yarn workspace command runs a turbo script that doesn't easily accept arbitrary arguments, so I need to find a way to ensure the dev server listens on port 8080 as expected.

Looking at the setup more carefully, I see that Next.js respects the PORT environment variable, so setting that should work. I'm also wondering if turbopack might cause issues in this environment and whether we need to disable it.

The postinstall scripts should run automatically during yarn install, which will generate the Prisma schema. I need to make sure NEXTAUTH_SECRET is set since the auth system requires it, and I should verify whether the web app actually needs Redis or if it can fall back gracefully.

The bigger concern is whether yarn install will complete in a reasonable timeframe given how large this monorepo is. I'll just write out the compose file and test it to see what happens.

Looking at what's actually required: the database is essential, but I don't think we need the API v2 service, Redis, or mailhog just to get the landing page running. Since we're using network_mode: host, the Postgres container will bind directly to the host's port 5432, and the DATABASE_URL can use 127.0.0.1 since the container's localhost is the same as the host's localhost.

content.thinking:
I'm understanding how the postinstall script chains together—husky sets up its hooks directory, then turbo runs the prisma generation and formatting tasks, which should all work fine in Docker. Now I need to think through the compose file structure, particularly around network configuration and whether to expose ports, since I'm re-reading the requirements to make sure I'm not missing any constraints.

Looking at the requirements more carefully, `network_mode: host` is definitely required, and with host networking I don't need to define published ports anyway. So I'm designing the compose file with postgres and web services, both using host networking, where postgres runs with a health check and the web service mounts the workspace and node_modules, sets the necessary database URLs and port, and waits for postgres to be healthy before starting.

Now I need to refine the database authentication — since I'm using `POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD: trust`, there's no password required, so the connection string should just be `postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso` without credentials. For the node_modules situation in this monorepo, I'm realizing that trying to mount individual volumes for each workspace's node_modules would be too complex. Instead, I'll let Yarn install everything fresh inside the container and persist it through the bind mount of the workspace itself. The host won't interfere with the container's node_modules, and Yarn 4 should handle the installation fine even though there might be minor differences between the container's Node version and what's on the host.

Now I'm thinking through the environment variables Cal.com needs to run — things like `NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL`, `NEXTAUTH_URL`, `NEXTAUTH_SECRET`, `CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY`, and `DATABASE_URL` are all required. Given how complex this monorepo is with its many dependencies, the initial Yarn install could easily take 10-20 minutes, so I should set up the compose file properly and then attempt to validate it, noting any blockers if they come up.

content.text: Now I have a good understanding. Let me design the compose file and create the configurations.

input.content:
# Alloy dev-env Docker Compose for Cal.diy
#
# Boots the smallest set of services needed to render the Cal.diy
# Next.js frontend at http://localhost:8080 inside the Alloy sandbox.
#
# Services:
#   - postgres: Postgres 18 backing store on host's :5432
#   - web:      @calcom/web Next.js dev server on host's :8080
#
# All services use host networking so they can talk to each other via
# 127.0.0.1 the same way the Alloy proxy reaches the frontend.

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    network_mode: host
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: calendso
      POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD: trust
    volumes:
      - alloy-postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres -d calendso -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5432"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 60
    restart: unless-stopped

  web:
    image: node:20-bookworm
    working_dir: /workspace
    network_mode: host
    volumes:
      - .:/workspace
      - alloy-yarn-cache:/usr/local/share/.cache/yarn
    environment:
      # --- Database ---
      DATABASE_URL: "postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso"
      DATABASE_DIRECT_URL: "postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso"
      PGSSLMODE: ""

      # --- Public URLs ---
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_API_V2_URL: ""
      NEXT_PUBLIC_EMBED_LIB_URL: "http://localhost:8080/embed/embed.js"
      ALLOWED_HOSTNAMES: '"localhost:8080","127.0.0.1:8080","localhost","127.0.0.1"'

      # --- NextAuth ---
      NEXTAUTH_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXTAUTH_SECRET: "alloy_dev_only_nextauth_secret_change_me_xxxxx"
      NEXTAUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN: ""

      # --- Cal.com required encryption key (must be 24 bytes base64) ---
      CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY: "ZGV2X2FsbG95X2VuY3J5cHRpb25fZGV2X3Nl"

      # --- Telemetry / branding ---
      CALCOM_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: "1"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_NAME: "Cal.diy"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_COMPANY_NAME: "Cal.com, Inc."
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPPORT_MAIL_ADDRESS: "help@cal.diy"

      # --- Email (no-op in dev) ---
      EMAIL_FROM: "notifications@cal.diy"
      EMAIL_FROM_NAME: "Cal.diy"
      EMAIL_SERVER_HOST: "127.0.0.1"
      EMAIL_SERVER_PORT: "1025"

      # --- Cron / misc ---
      CRON_API_KEY: "alloy_dev_cron_key"
      CRON_ENABLE_APP_SYNC: "false"
      API_KEY_PREFIX: "cal_"

      # --- Next.js dev server port ---
      PORT: "8080"
      HOST: "0.0.0.0"
      HOSTNAME: "0.0.0.0"

      # --- Memory / performance ---
      NODE_OPTIONS: "--max-old-space-size=8192"
      NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: "1"
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -euo pipefail
        echo "[alloy] enabling corepack / yarn 4..."
        corepack enable
        corepack prepare yarn@4.12.0 --activate

        if [ ! -d node_modules ] || [ ! -d apps/web/node_modules ]; then
          echo "[alloy] running yarn install (first boot — may take a while)..."
          yarn install --inline-builds
        else
          echo "[alloy] node_modules present — skipping yarn install"
        fi

        echo "[alloy] applying prisma migrations..."
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma prisma migrate deploy

        echo "[alloy] seeding app store + basic data (best effort)..."
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-app-store || echo "[alloy] seed-app-store failed (non-fatal)"
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-basic || echo "[alloy] seed-basic failed (non-fatal)"

        echo "[alloy] starting Next.js dev server on :8080..."
        cd apps/web
        exec yarn dev --port 8080 --hostname 0.0.0.0
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  alloy-postgres-data:
  alloy-yarn-cache:

content.content: File created successfully at: /workspace/docker-compose.alloy.yaml

# Alloy dev-env Docker Compose for Cal.diy
#
# Boots the smallest set of services needed to render the Cal.diy
# Next.js frontend at http://localhost:8080 inside the Alloy sandbox.
#
# Services:
#   - postgres: Postgres 18 backing store on host's :5432
#   - web:      @calcom/web Next.js dev server on host's :8080
#
# All services use host networking so they can talk to each other via
# 127.0.0.1 the same way the Alloy proxy reaches the frontend.

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    network_mode: host
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: calendso
      POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD: trust
    volumes:
      - alloy-postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres -d calendso -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5432"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 60
    restart: unless-stopped

  web:
    image: node:20-bookworm
    working_dir: /workspace
    network_mode: host
    volumes:
      - .:/workspace
      - alloy-yarn-cache:/usr/local/share/.cache/yarn
    environment:
      # --- Database ---
      DATABASE_URL: "postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso"
      DATABASE_DIRECT_URL: "postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso"
      PGSSLMODE: ""

      # --- Public URLs ---
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_API_V2_URL: ""
      NEXT_PUBLIC_EMBED_LIB_URL: "http://localhost:8080/embed/embed.js"
      ALLOWED_HOSTNAMES: '"localhost:8080","127.0.0.1:8080","localhost","127.0.0.1"'

      # --- NextAuth ---
      NEXTAUTH_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXTAUTH_SECRET: "alloy_dev_only_nextauth_secret_change_me_xxxxx"
      NEXTAUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN: ""

      # --- Cal.com required encryption key (must be 24 bytes base64) ---
      CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY: "ZGV2X2FsbG95X2VuY3J5cHRpb25fZGV2X3Nl"

      # --- Telemetry / branding ---
      CALCOM_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: "1"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_NAME: "Cal.diy"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_COMPANY_NAME: "Cal.com, Inc."
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPPORT_MAIL_ADDRESS: "help@cal.diy"

      # --- Email (no-op in dev) ---
      EMAIL_FROM: "notifications@cal.diy"
      EMAIL_FROM_NAME: "Cal.diy"
      EMAIL_SERVER_HOST: "127.0.0.1"
      EMAIL_SERVER_PORT: "1025"

      # --- Cron / misc ---
      CRON_API_KEY: "alloy_dev_cron_key"
      CRON_ENABLE_APP_SYNC: "false"
      API_KEY_PREFIX: "cal_"

      # --- Next.js dev server port ---
      PORT: "8080"
      HOST: "0.0.0.0"
      HOSTNAME: "0.0.0.0"

      # --- Memory / performance ---
      NODE_OPTIONS: "--max-old-space-size=8192"
      NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: "1"
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -euo pipefail
        echo "[alloy] enabling corepack / yarn 4..."
        corepack enable
        corepack prepare yarn@4.12.0 --activate

        if [ ! -d node_modules ] || [ ! -d apps/web/node_modules ]; then
          echo "[alloy] running yarn install (first boot — may take a while)..."
          yarn install --inline-builds
        else
          echo "[alloy] node_modules present — skipping yarn install"
        fi

        echo "[alloy] applying prisma migrations..."
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma prisma migrate deploy

        echo "[alloy] seeding app store + basic data (best effort)..."
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-app-store || echo "[alloy] seed-app-store failed (non-fatal)"
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-basic || echo "[alloy] seed-basic failed (non-fatal)"

        echo "[alloy] starting Next.js dev server on :8080..."
        cd apps/web
        exec yarn dev --port 8080 --hostname 0.0.0.0
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  alloy-postgres-data:
  alloy-yarn-cache:

# Alloy dev-env Docker Compose for Cal.diy
#
# Boots the smallest set of services needed to render the Cal.diy
# Next.js frontend at http://localhost:8080 inside the Alloy sandbox.
#
# Services:
#   - postgres: Postgres 18 backing store on host's :5432
#   - web:      @calcom/web Next.js dev server on host's :8080
#
# All services use host networking so they can talk to each other via
# 127.0.0.1 the same way the Alloy proxy reaches the frontend.

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    network_mode: host
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: calendso
      POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD: trust
    volumes:
      - alloy-postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres -d calendso -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5432"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 60
    restart: unless-stopped

  web:
    image: node:20-bookworm
    working_dir: /workspace
    network_mode: host
    volumes:
      - .:/workspace
      - alloy-yarn-cache:/usr/local/share/.cache/yarn
    environment:
      # --- Database ---
      DATABASE_URL: "postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso"
      DATABASE_DIRECT_URL: "postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso"
      PGSSLMODE: ""

      # --- Public URLs ---
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_API_V2_URL: ""
      NEXT_PUBLIC_EMBED_LIB_URL: "http://localhost:8080/embed/embed.js"
      ALLOWED_HOSTNAMES: '"localhost:8080","127.0.0.1:8080","localhost","127.0.0.1"'

      # --- NextAuth ---
      NEXTAUTH_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXTAUTH_SECRET: "alloy_dev_only_nextauth_secret_change_me_xxxxx"
      NEXTAUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN: ""

      # --- Cal.com required encryption key (must be 24 bytes base64) ---
      CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY: "ZGV2X2FsbG95X2VuY3J5cHRpb25fZGV2X3Nl"

      # --- Telemetry / branding ---
      CALCOM_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: "1"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_NAME: "Cal.diy"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_COMPANY_NAME: "Cal.com, Inc."
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPPORT_MAIL_ADDRESS: "help@cal.diy"

      # --- Email (no-op in dev) ---
      EMAIL_FROM: "notifications@cal.diy"
      EMAIL_FROM_NAME: "Cal.diy"
      EMAIL_SERVER_HOST: "127.0.0.1"
      EMAIL_SERVER_PORT: "1025"

      # --- Cron / misc ---
      CRON_API_KEY: "alloy_dev_cron_key"
      CRON_ENABLE_APP_SYNC: "false"
      API_KEY_PREFIX: "cal_"

      # --- Next.js dev server port ---
      PORT: "8080"
      HOST: "0.0.0.0"
      HOSTNAME: "0.0.0.0"

      # --- Memory / performance ---
      NODE_OPTIONS: "--max-old-space-size=8192"
      NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: "1"
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -euo pipefail
        echo "[alloy] enabling corepack / yarn 4..."
        corepack enable
        corepack prepare yarn@4.12.0 --activate

        if [ ! -d node_modules ] || [ ! -d apps/web/node_modules ]; then
          echo "[alloy] running yarn install (first boot — may take a while)..."
          yarn install --inline-builds
        else
          echo "[alloy] node_modules present — skipping yarn install"
        fi

        echo "[alloy] applying prisma migrations..."
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma prisma migrate deploy

        echo "[alloy] seeding app store + basic data (best effort)..."
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-app-store || echo "[alloy] seed-app-store failed (non-fatal)"
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-basic || echo "[alloy] seed-basic failed (non-fatal)"

        echo "[alloy] starting Next.js dev server on :8080..."
        cd apps/web
        exec yarn dev --port 8080 --hostname 0.0.0.0
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  alloy-postgres-data:
  alloy-yarn-cache:

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
chore(alloy): add Alloy dev-env workspace setup

Boots the @calcom/web Next.js dev server at http://localhost:8080 in
the Alloy sandbox using a minimal two-service stack:

- postgres: Postgres 18 backing the Prisma schema + seed data
- web:      runs yarn install, prisma migrate deploy, app-store seed,
            and `next dev --port 8080` against the bind-mounted repo

All services use `network_mode: host` so they reach each other (and
the Alloy proxy) via 127.0.0.1 without published ports.

sets `homeUrl` to `/auth/login`, the page Cal.diy redirects to for
unauthenticated users.

content.text:
Workspace is set up and verified. Here's the summary:

**What was added (committed to `slavko-bojanic/alloy-setup-our-workspace-please-mnz4fC`):**

- **`docker-compose.alloy.yaml`** — minimal two-service stack:
  - `postgres` (Postgres 18, trust auth, persistent volume)
  - `web` (Node 20, runs `yarn install` → `prisma migrate deploy` → app-store seed → `next dev --port 8080`)
  - Both use `network_mode: host`; no published `ports`
- **`.alloy/environment.json`** — points at `docker-compose.alloy.yaml`, with `homeUrl: "/auth/login"` (Cal.diy redirects there for unauthenticated users)

**Validation:** Stack came up cleanly. Playwright navigated to `http://localhost:8080`, was redirected to `/auth/login`, and rendered the **Cal.diy login page** (Email + Password fields, "Continue", "Create account", title `Login | Cal.diy`). Console shows only routine HMR / Fast Refresh logs and a benign `react-i18next` warning — no fatal errors.

**Notes for follow-up sessions:**
- First boot is slow (~3 min): yarn install (3578 packages, ~760 MB) + prisma migrations + seed. Subsequent boots reuse `node_modules` from the bind mount, so the script skips reinstalling and goes straight to `next dev`.
- `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` and `CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY` are dev-only placeholder values — fine for a sandbox, swap before any real use.
- API v2 (NestJS), Redis, and Mailhog are intentionally omitted — the web app boots without them and the home/login flow does not require them.

content.text:
Yes — `X-Frame-Options: DENY` is set, but only on two specific route patterns in `apps/web/next.config.ts:388-406`:

| Path | Header |
|---|---|
| `/auth/:path*` (login, signup, forgot-password, etc.) | `X-Frame-Options: DENY` |
| `/signup` | `X-Frame-Options: DENY` |

Everywhere else (including `/`, dashboards, booking pages, embeds) intentionally does **not** set `X-Frame-Options`, because Cal.diy's whole product is built around being embedded in third-party sites. The catch-all `/:path*` rule only sets `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` and `Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin`.

So yes — DENY is set, but scoped to auth/signup pages only. That's relevant for our Alloy setup: the proxy lands on `/auth/login` (the homeUrl I configured), which means the page is served with `X-Frame-Options: DENY`. If the Alloy preview embeds the page in an iframe, the login screen will refuse to render. Want me to either:

1. Change `homeUrl` to a non-auth route (e.g. `/event-types`, which would itself redirect to `/auth/login` but without DENY on the redirected response chain — though browsers apply XFO on the final response, so this likely doesn't help)
2. Add a `loginScript` that auto-fills `pro@example.com` / `pro` so we land on a frameable page after login
3. Leave as-is and have you preview in a regular browser tab

Let me know which fits your workflow.

content.text:
Done. Both X-Frame-Options DENY headers (on `/auth/:path*` and `/signup`) are removed from `apps/web/next.config.ts` and committed as `4e29d6d237` on the alloy branch.

Runtime verified — `curl -I http://localhost:8080/auth/login` and `/signup` no longer return an `X-Frame-Options` header. The catch-all `/:path*` rule keeps `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff` and `Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin` in place, and there's no CSP `frame-ancestors` directive elsewhere, so the auth pages are now embeddable in the Alloy preview iframe.

Worth noting: the prior commit (`ce6d7f92cf Add X-Frame-Options: DENY header`) deliberately added these headers, so this is a real product trade-off — auth pages can now be framed by anything, which makes clickjacking attacks against login/signup theoretically possible. Fine for the Alloy sandbox; you'd want to think twice before shipping it to a real deployment.

lastPrompt: How can we get to an actual authenticated screen without the auth flow here?    <mode>ACT</mode>

content.thinking:
I'm realizing the credentials provider is registered under the `credentials` id, so I can either do a programmatic login by hitting the CSRF endpoint and then posting to the callback route with the token and credentials, or I could just submit the existing form directly since that's simpler.

For the Alloy preview, I'm weighing two approaches: one where a login script auto-fills and submits the form, but that's tricky with react-hook-form since it needs proper event dispatching rather than just setting values directly. The other option is having the script make an API call directly to get the CSRF token and then post the credentials to the NextAuth callback endpoint, which bypasses the form entirely and checks if the session cookie already exists to avoid redundant logins.

I'm leaning toward a simpler hybrid approach: create a dev-only API route that handles the authentication server-side, so the login script just needs to navigate to it. This route would look up the pro user, create a JWT session token using NextAuth's signing mechanism with the NEXTAUTH_SECRET, and redirect to the dashboard. That way the client-side script stays minimal and we avoid form interaction complexity altogether.

Building the dev API route...

input.function: async () => { try { if (document.cookie.split(';').some(function (c) { return c.trim().split('=')[0].endsWith('next-auth.session-token'); })) { return 'already logged in'; } var csrf = await (await fetch('/api/auth/csrf', { credentials: 'include' })).json(); var body = new URLSearchParams({ csrfToken: csrf.csrfToken, email: 'pro@example.com', password: 'pro', callbackUrl: '/event-types', json: 'true' }); var res = await fetch('/api/auth/callback/credentials', { method: 'POST', credentials: 'include', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' }, body: body.toString() }); if (res.ok) { var data = await res.json().catch(function () { return {}; }); window.location.replace(data && data.url ? data.url : '/event-types'); return 'logged in via ' + (data && data.url || '/event-types'); } else { return 'failed: ' + res.status; } } catch (e) { return 'error: ' + e.message; } }

diff --git a/.alloy/environment.json b/.alloy/environment.json
index 733ece4351..d870354365 100644
--- a/.alloy/environment.json
+++ b/.alloy/environment.json
@@ -1,4 +1,5 @@
 {
   "dockerComposePath": "docker-compose.alloy.yaml",
-  "homeUrl": "/auth/login"
+  "homeUrl": "/auth/login",
+  "loginScript": "(async () => { try { if (document.cookie.split(';').some(function (c) { return c.trim().split('=')[0].endsWith('next-auth.session-token'); })) { return; } var csrf = await (await fetch('/api/auth/csrf', { credentials: 'include' })).json(); var body = new URLSearchParams({ csrfToken: csrf.csrfToken, email: 'pro@example.com', password: 'pro', callbackUrl: '/event-types', json: 'true' }); var res = await fetch('/api/auth/callback/credentials', { method: 'POST', credentials: 'include', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' }, body: body.toString() }); if (res.ok) { var data = await res.json().catch(function () { return {}; }); window.location.replace(data && data.url ? data.url : '/event-types'); } } catch (e) { console.error('[alloy] auto-login failed', e); } })();"
 }

input.command:
git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
chore(alloy): auto-login as pro@example.com via loginScript

Adds a `loginScript` to .alloy/environment.json that runs once in the
proxied app page. If no NextAuth session cookie is present, it:

  1. fetches /api/auth/csrf for the csrfToken
  2. POSTs csrfToken + pro@example.com / pro to
     /api/auth/callback/credentials with json=true
  3. on 200 OK, redirects to the returned callbackUrl (/event-types)

This relies on the seeded `pro` user from `yarn workspace
@calcom/prisma seed-basic` (already part of our compose boot
sequence) and on the NextAuth credentials provider configured in
packages/features/auth/lib/next-auth-options.ts.

Verified end-to-end against the running stack: navigated to
/auth/login, ran the script, landed on /event-types fully rendered
with the seeded pro user's event list.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

content.text:
Done. Here's what I added and how it works:

**The mechanism** — a `loginScript` in `.alloy/environment.json` that the Alloy proxy injects into the app page once:

1. Skip if a `next-auth.session-token` cookie already exists (idempotent on refresh).
2. `GET /api/auth/csrf` → grab the `csrfToken`.
3. `POST /api/auth/callback/credentials` with `csrfToken`, `email=pro@example.com`, `password=pro`, `callbackUrl=/event-types`, `json=true`. NextAuth verifies the credentials, signs a JWT, and returns `Set-Cookie: next-auth.session-token=…`.
4. On 200 OK, `window.location.replace(...)` to `/event-types`.

**End-to-end verification** — ran the exact same script in the live page via Playwright, landed on **Event types | Cal.diy** with the pro user's seeded event list (Tennis class, Yoga class, Google Meet, Zoom, Daily, etc.) and full sidebar nav. Screenshot in the previous turn.

**Why this approach over the alternatives:**
- **No new code paths in the app** — it uses the same NextAuth credentials endpoint the real login form uses, so it can't drift from prod auth behavior.
- **Pure config change** — lives in `.alloy/environment.json`, doesn't touch the codebase.
- **Survives a `homeUrl` change** — if you later set `homeUrl: "/event-types"`, the script still runs there, sees no cookie, logs in, and reloads.

**Caveats:**
- Depends on `pro@example.com` existing in the DB. Our compose runs `yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-basic` on first boot, which creates it. If you ever swap to a fresh DB volume, that seed must run before the script will succeed.
- Adds a brief flash of the login page before the redirect kicks in. Acceptable for a dev sandbox; not something you'd ship.
- Credentials are checked-into the repo. Fine because `pro` / `pro` is a well-known seed credential, not a real account.

content.thinking:
So the user is hitting a redirect loop when they try to access `/event-types` after login, whether they click Continue or navigate manually. The key detail I'm missing is that they're going through the Alloy proxy rather than hitting the server directly—that could be changing how the session or cookies are being handled.

The session cookie is set as host-only with no explicit domain, so if the proxy URL doesn't match `NEXTAUTH_URL`, the cookie might not be sent back on subsequent requests. And when NextAuth validates the callback URL after login, it checks against allowed origins—if the proxy URL doesn't match what's configured, the redirect could fail and loop back to login. and the proxy is serving it over HTTPS, so the browser should include the `next-auth.session-token` cookie in subsequent requests to the proxy domain. The real issue might be that the session validation on the server side is failing because it's checking the request host or some other mismatch between what the proxy presents and what the backend expects.

Actually, I think the problem is in the SSR validation — after the redirect to `/event-types` succeeds, the `getServerSession` call during page render might be failing its own checks, possibly because the request context or headers don't match what the session validation expects.

content.thinking:
The login works fine when accessing localhost:8080 directly, but when going through the Alloy proxy at `https://*.alloy.dev/...`, the Continue button redirects back to the login page instead of proceeding. I'm trying to figure out what's different about the proxy setup that's breaking the authentication flow.

The issue is likely that NEXTAUTH_URL is hardcoded to `http://localhost:8080`, which NextAuth uses to determine cookie settings, generate redirect URLs, and validate callback URLs. When the user accesses through the proxy on HTTPS, NextAuth returns redirect URLs pointing back to `http://localhost:8080`, which either fails to resolve or escapes the proxy entirely. Additionally, the cookie security flags and the mismatch between what the form expects and what the auth callback returns are probably causing the redirect loop. The core issue is that NextAuth's default behavior validates the callback URL against NEXTAUTH_URL, and since that's set to localhost:8080 while the user is accessing through the proxy, the server returns the localhost URL. When the client then tries to navigate to that absolute URL via router.push, it breaks out of the proxy iframe and fails. The fix needs to ensure the callback URL stays within the proxy's origin. in which case no cookie was sent because cookies were on proxy host). So the user's redirect back to login suggests either the POST failed silently or the navigation succeeded but hit an unauthenticated route that redirected them back. Let me think through what NextAuth actually returns here — with NEXTAUTH_URL mismatched from the request origin, NextAuth might reject the callback entirely or fall back to trusting the request host. In v4 with `trustHost: true`, it uses the canonical NEXTAUTH_URL, which could cause the session to fail. The cleanest fix would be making the app use relative URLs throughout, but since cal.com relies heavily on WEBAPP_URL, that's not practical. The loginScript approach we already have works because it uses `window.location.replace` with the returned URL, so maybe that's the path forward here.

Actually, wait — my Playwright test passed because it was hitting localhost directly, not through the proxy. So the loginScript would also break for proxy users since it's still trying to navigate to `http://localhost:8080/event-types`. The real issue is that NextAuth's callback needs to return either a relative URL or a URL on the proxy's origin. If we change the callback to use relative paths, NextAuth will still resolve them against NEXTAUTH_URL and return the localhost version. The only truly clean solution is making NEXTAUTH_URL match what the user actually sees, but since the proxy URL isn't known at startup, we'd need runtime detection or Auth.js v5's trustHost support.

Cal.com is on NextAuth v4 though, so we're limited to options like `useSecureCookies: false` or cookie overrides. But there's also NEXTAUTH_URL_INTERNAL for internal calls versus NEXTAUTH_URL for external ones — though that still doesn't solve the cookie domain issue.

Let me reconsider what the user actually described: clicking Continue redirects back to login, and manually navigating to /event-types also redirects to login. That points to the session not persisting at all. My Playwright test worked because the cookies stayed in the session, but through the proxy, the flow might be: the login callback sets a cookie on the proxy origin, then when /event-types is requested, the backend reads the session token cookie and validates it with NEXTAUTH_SECRET — so the real question is whether that validation is actually succeeding.

A few possibilities: the proxy could be stripping cookies entirely, or NextAuth's JWT verification might be failing because of a Host header mismatch, or if the app is iframed in the preview, SameSite cookie restrictions could prevent the cookie from being sent on cross-site navigations. But wait — in the Alloy preview, the proxy serves the whole thing, so it should be same-site. Let me think about whether the cookie is even being set in the first place, or if there's something about how the preview environment handles cookies that I'm missing.

Actually, I should focus on what I can actually fix: the most likely issue is that NextAuth requires `NEXTAUTH_URL` to match the request origin, and either the login response sets cookies tied to `http://localhost:8080` or the JWT is signed with that audience, causing validation to fail on the proxy host. I should check if NextAuth supports `NEXTAUTH_URL_INTERNAL` or `AUTH_TRUST_HOST` to make it work across different hosts.

content.content:
1	import type { CookieOption, CookiesOptions } from "next-auth";
2	
3	/**
4	 * Copy from 'https://github.com/nextauthjs/next-auth/blob/227ff2259f/src/core/lib/cookie.ts' as we can't import it directly
5	 *
6	 * Use secure cookies if the site uses HTTPS
7	 * This being conditional allows cookies to work non-HTTPS development URLs
8	 * Honour secure cookie option, which sets 'secure' and also adds '__Secure-'
9	 * prefix, but enable them by default if the site URL is HTTPS; but not for
10	 * non-HTTPS URLs like http://localhost which are used in development).
11	 * For more on prefixes see https://googlechrome.github.io/samples/cookie-prefixes/
12	 *
13	 */
14	
15	const NEXTAUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN = process.env.NEXTAUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN || "";
16	
17	export function defaultCookies(useSecureCookies: boolean): CookiesOptions {
18	  const cookiePrefix = useSecureCookies ? "__Secure-" : "";
19	
20	  const defaultOptions: CookieOption["options"] = {
21	    domain: NEXTAUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN || undefined,
22	    // To enable cookies on widgets,
23	    // https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45094712/iframe-not-reading-cookies-in-chrome
24	    // But we need to set it as `lax` in development
25	    sameSite: useSecureCookies ? "none" : "lax",
26	    path: "/",
27	    secure: useSecureCookies,
28	  };
29	  return {
30	    sessionToken: {
31	      name: `${cookiePrefix}next-auth.session-token`,
32	      options: {
33	        ...defaultOptions,
34	        httpOnly: true,
35	      },
36	    },
37	    callbackUrl: {
38	      name: `${cookiePrefix}next-auth.callback-url`,
39	      options: defaultOptions,
40	    },
41	    csrfToken: {
42	      name: `${cookiePrefix}next-auth.csrf-token`,
43	      options: {
44	        ...defaultOptions,
45	        httpOnly: true,
46	      },
47	    },
48	    pkceCodeVerifier: {

content.thinking:
I found the issue — `useSecureCookies` is based on whether `WEBAPP_URL` starts with "https://", and since it's set to `http://localhost:8080`, secure cookies are disabled, which means the session token cookie is being set with `secure: false` and `sameSite: "lax"`.

The real problem is that when the Alloy preview serves over HTTPS and proxies the app, the browser receives the cookie over a secure connection but without the `Secure` flag. While modern browsers allow this, the `SameSite=Lax` policy becomes problematic in an iframe context — if the preview shell and the proxied app iframe have different origins, Chrome treats requests from the iframe as cross-site, which means the `SameSite=Lax` cookie won't be sent along with those requests.

The solution would be to set `NEXTAUTH_URL` to an HTTPS value so that `useSecureCookies` becomes true, which would add the `Secure` flag and change the policy to `SameSite=None`. But this creates a new problem: if we set `NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL=https://localhost:8080`, the browser would reject `Secure` cookies when accessing the app directly over HTTP, breaking that access path. We need a way to make cookies work in both the proxied HTTPS context and direct HTTP localhost access.

The most practical approaches are either to run the dev stack over HTTPS entirely, add a middleware layer that forces `SameSite=None; Secure` on all Set-Cookie headers, or override the `useSecureCookies` logic directly in the code to always enable secure cookies regardless of the URL scheme.

I could also add an HTTPS-terminating reverse proxy in the docker-compose setup, but that adds complexity. Let me step back and focus on what the user is actually experiencing — they're getting redirected to login after clicking Continue, which suggests the session cookie isn't persisting in the Alloy preview context. Rather than keep guessing at solutions, I should look at the actual response headers to see what cookie is being set and understand the exact context where it's failing.

content.thinking:
I found the issue—the session cookie is missing the `Secure` flag and only has `SameSite=Lax`, which means it won't be sent in cross-site iframe requests. Since the Alloy preview likely serves the proxied app over HTTPS in an iframe from a different origin, the cookies need to be marked as `Secure` to work properly in that context.

The problem traces back to `useSecureCookies` being determined by whether `WEBAPP_URL` starts with `https://`, but setting that to HTTPS breaks direct localhost access since browsers reject Secure cookies over HTTP. The real issue is that the app generates absolute URLs using `WEBAPP_URL`, so if I set it to `https://localhost:8080`, those URLs won't actually work for direct access—though in the Alloy preview, users only access through the proxy anyway, so the internal localhost URL is irrelevant there.

I'm weighing a few options: forcing `WEBAPP_URL` to HTTPS (breaks direct access), overriding the cookie defaults in code, or adding a simple environment variable like `FORCE_SECURE_COOKIES=true` to patch the default-cookies logic without changing the URL. The env var approach seems cleanest—just a one-line modification to the default-cookies file.

But I should step back and verify whether the user actually has an iframe situation or if something else is going on with the auth flow.

The likely scenario is that after successful login, NextAuth returns a redirect URL like `http://localhost:8080/event-types`, and the router pushes to that absolute URL. The browser then tries to navigate to localhost:8080 directly, which either fails or gets intercepted by the proxy. The session cookie was set on the proxy origin, so when the request eventually reaches the backend through the proxy, the session should be readable—but the user ends up back at login, suggesting the cookie isn't being sent or recognized on the subsequent request.

The core issue is those absolute URLs forcing the browser away from the proxy origin. Looking at the login view code, it's already trying to handle callback URLs by checking if they're relative or absolute and prepending the webapp URL when needed.

The pragmatic fix is simpler than trying to reconfigure the entire auth setup: I should patch the login view to use relative URLs directly instead of forcing absolute ones, and also address the SameSite cookie issue for the dev environment. Setting `NEXTAUTH_URL` to handle wildcard origins would help too, but the immediate win is just making those redirects stay on whatever origin the user's already on. to navigate through — like making it relative or using a proxy-aware redirect that doesn't break the user's browser navigation from the HTTPS proxy back to localhost HTTP.

The real issue is that NextAuth's redirect callback expects an absolute URL built from NEXTAUTH_URL, so if I pass a relative path, it'll still prepend the wrong base. The cleanest fix is to set NEXTAUTH_URL to something the proxy can actually resolve, but the Alloy proxy doesn't expose its own URL as an environment variable, so that's not viable either.

I think the pragmatic solution here is to configure NEXTAUTH_URL to match what the proxy presents to the user's browser, or patch the redirect logic to detect when we're behind a proxy and handle relative URLs appropriately without breaking the callback flow. If we set NEXTAUTH_URL to a relative-ish "/" prefix... NextAuth might choke.

Actually, looking at the user's situation more: I bet the Alloy proxy sets a NEXTAUTH_URL env variable in the form of `https://*.alloy.dev`. But it doesn't.

OK let me just try the most targeted fix and see how far it gets us.

**Plan:**
1. Modify `defaultCookies` to optionally set `SameSite=None; Secure` via an env var like `NEXTAUTH_FORCE_SECURE_COOKIES=true`. Default behavior unchanged.
2. Modify login-view.tsx to use relative URLs: change `router.push(callbackUrl)` to use the unprefixed path.
3. Also adjust the `loginScript` to use relative paths.
4. Set NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL accordingly

Actually wait. A simpler approach: we set NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL to use `https://localhost:8080`. That makes:
- Cookies: SameSite=None; Secure; __Secure-prefix
- callbackUrl: "https://localhost:8080/event-types"

The user accessing through the proxy would get `window.location.assign("https://localhost:8080/event-types")` — still fails for the same reason: browser tries to go to localhost:8080.

So changing scheme doesn't fix the absolute URL navigation issue. The real fix is making the URL not absolute or making it relative to the proxy origin. I should patch `login-view.tsx` to use relative URLs since the login flow is the immediate problem.

Wait, let me reconsider what the user meant by "redirects me back to the login page." If the form submission succeeds, it calls `router.push(callbackUrl)` with an absolute URL like `https://localhost:8080/event-types`. The browser tries to navigate there, but from the user's machine that either fails or hits a different backend without the cookie, which then redirects back to `/auth/login`. So the user ends up back on the login page they're actually seeing through the proxy.

The CSRF token issue is likely the culprit: when the form POSTs from an iframe context, the SameSite=Lax cookie set in the CSRF fetch might not be sent back in the POST request, causing NextAuth to reject the login. Setting cookies to `SameSite=None; Secure` would fix that, but the real solution is making sure the redirect after login uses a relative URL so it stays within the proxy context.

I'm planning to patch the cookie configuration to auto-enable secure cookies when NEXTAUTH_URL uses HTTPS, and then modify the login view to strip the WEBAPP_URL prefix and use relative paths for redirects instead of absolute URLs. The redirect after login is hardcoded to use an absolute URL, which breaks when accessed through the proxy iframe—it tries to navigate to localhost:8080 instead of staying within the proxy context. I need to check what `getSafeRedirectUrl` returns and potentially switch to relative URLs to keep navigation within the current origin.

The session cookie isn't being set because either the CSRF validation itself is blocked in the iframe context, or the subsequent session cookie can't be sent back due to SameSite restrictions. The real problem is that the callback URL is absolute (`http://localhost:8080/event-types`), which breaks the user out of the proxy entirely instead of staying within the iframe.

The solution is to configure NextAuth cookies with `SameSite=None; Secure` so they work across iframe boundaries, and switch to relative callback URLs to keep navigation within the proxied context. So the core issue is that when the iframe navigates to an absolute URL with a different origin, it breaks out of the proxy. I need to convert the callback URL to a relative path in login-view so router.push stays within the same origin, and also ensure the cookie security settings work correctly with the environment variable override.

content.text:
I have the diagnosis. Let me explain, then fix:

**Two compounding bugs that hit you through the Alloy proxy (but not on direct `localhost:8080`):**

1. **Cookies are set as `SameSite=Lax`, no `Secure`**. The Alloy proxy serves the app over HTTPS inside a cross-site iframe. Browsers refuse to send `SameSite=Lax` cookies on requests issued from a cross-site iframe context. So either the CSRF cookie doesn't reach `/api/auth/callback/credentials` (login silently fails) or the session cookie doesn't reach `/event-types` on the next request (auth gate redirects to `/auth/login`). My direct-localhost Playwright test passed because everything is same-site there.

2. **`router.push(callbackUrl)` pushes the absolute URL `http://localhost:8080/event-types`** (built from `NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL`). Next.js falls back to `window.location.assign()` for cross-origin URLs, so the browser tries to leave the proxy and load `localhost:8080` directly from the user's machine — where nothing's listening.

Let me fix both:

+// cookies are not sent on subresource requests issued from cross-site iframes.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Alloy dev-env Docker Compose for Cal.diy
#
# Boots the smallest set of services needed to render the Cal.diy
# Next.js frontend at http://localhost:8080 inside the Alloy sandbox.
#
# Services:
#   - postgres: Postgres 18 backing store on host's :5432
#   - web:      @calcom/web Next.js dev server on host's :8080
#
# All services use host networking so they can talk to each other via
# 127.0.0.1 the same way the Alloy proxy reaches the frontend.

services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:18
    network_mode: host
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: postgres
      POSTGRES_DB: calendso
      POSTGRES_HOST_AUTH_METHOD: trust
    volumes:
      - alloy-postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres -d calendso -h 127.0.0.1 -p 5432"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 60
    restart: unless-stopped

  web:
    image: node:20-bookworm
    working_dir: /workspace
    network_mode: host
    volumes:
      - .:/workspace
      - alloy-yarn-cache:/usr/local/share/.cache/yarn
    environment:
      # --- Database ---
      DATABASE_URL: "postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso"
      DATABASE_DIRECT_URL: "postgresql://postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/calendso"
      PGSSLMODE: ""

      # --- Public URLs ---
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBAPP_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBSITE_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_API_V2_URL: ""
      NEXT_PUBLIC_EMBED_LIB_URL: "http://localhost:8080/embed/embed.js"
      ALLOWED_HOSTNAMES: '"localhost:8080","127.0.0.1:8080","localhost","127.0.0.1"'

      # --- NextAuth ---
      NEXTAUTH_URL: "http://localhost:8080"
      NEXTAUTH_SECRET: "alloy_dev_only_nextauth_secret_change_me_xxxxx"
      NEXTAUTH_COOKIE_DOMAIN: ""

      # --- Cal.com required encryption key (must be 24 bytes base64) ---
      CALENDSO_ENCRYPTION_KEY: "ZGV2X2FsbG95X2VuY3J5cHRpb25fZGV2X3Nl"

      # --- Telemetry / branding ---
      CALCOM_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: "1"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_NAME: "Cal.diy"
      NEXT_PUBLIC_COMPANY_NAME: "Cal.com, Inc."
      NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPPORT_MAIL_ADDRESS: "help@cal.diy"

      # --- Email (no-op in dev) ---
      EMAIL_FROM: "notifications@cal.diy"
      EMAIL_FROM_NAME: "Cal.diy"
      EMAIL_SERVER_HOST: "127.0.0.1"
      EMAIL_SERVER_PORT: "1025"

      # --- Cron / misc ---
      CRON_API_KEY: "alloy_dev_cron_key"
      CRON_ENABLE_APP_SYNC: "false"
      API_KEY_PREFIX: "cal_"

      # --- Next.js dev server port ---
      PORT: "8080"
      HOST: "0.0.0.0"
      HOSTNAME: "0.0.0.0"

      # --- Memory / performance ---
      NODE_OPTIONS: "--max-old-space-size=8192"
      NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: "1"
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -euo pipefail
        echo "[alloy] enabling corepack / yarn 4..."
        corepack enable
        corepack prepare yarn@4.12.0 --activate

        if [ ! -d node_modules ] || [ ! -d apps/web/node_modules ]; then
          echo "[alloy] running yarn install (first boot — may take a while)..."
          yarn install --inline-builds
        else
          echo "[alloy] node_modules present — skipping yarn install"
        fi

        echo "[alloy] applying prisma migrations..."
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma prisma migrate deploy

        echo "[alloy] seeding app store + basic data (best effort)..."
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-app-store || echo "[alloy] seed-app-store failed (non-fatal)"
        yarn workspace @calcom/prisma seed-basic || echo "[alloy] seed-basic failed (non-fatal)"

        echo "[alloy] starting Next.js dev server on :8080..."
        cd apps/web
        exec yarn dev --port 8080 --hostname 0.0.0.0
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  alloy-postgres-data:
  alloy-yarn-cache:
